name: System design foundations
description: CAP theorem and classic design exercises (URL shortener, media streaming). From the 'system design' prep doc.

topics.name: CAP theorem
topics.description: Consistency, availability, partition tolerance — pick two under a network partition.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The CAP theorem says a distributed system can only guarantee two of three properties at once: Consistency (every read sees the latest write), Availability (every request gets an answer), and Partition tolerance (the system survives the network dropping messages between its nodes).

## What problem does it solve?

It kills a fantasy: that a distributed system can be always-consistent AND always-available, even when the network fails. It cannot — and the theorem tells you the exact choice you face.

## How it works

The key insight: network partitions are NOT optional. Cables break, switches die, cloud zones lose contact — so P is mandatory. The REAL choice appears during a partition, and it is C versus A:
- CP: refuse requests rather than serve possibly stale data. A bank ledger does this — better to reject a withdrawal than allow a double-spend.
- AP: keep answering, accepting that some answers may be stale, and reconcile later. A shopping cart does this — better to accept the item than block the customer.

The refinement worth naming — PACELC: even WITHOUT a partition there is a trade-off, between Latency and Consistency. Synchronous replication (wait for all copies to confirm) buys consistency with slowness; asynchronous replication buys speed with a staleness window.

The senior nuance: the choice is per OPERATION, not per company. The same product is CP for payments and AP for its dashboard — and saying so beats picking a single letter-pair for everything.

## Key terms

- Partition — nodes alive but unable to talk; the unavoidable P
- CP vs AP — refuse-to-be-wrong vs answer-possibly-stale
- PACELC — the everyday latency-vs-consistency trade-off
- Eventual consistency — AP's promise: stale now, correct soon

topics.name: URL shortener design
topics.description: Designing a bit.ly-style service: key generation, storage, redirects, scale.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The classic system-design interview question: build bit.ly — a service that turns long URLs into short codes and redirects visitors. Small enough to finish in 40 minutes, rich enough to test every fundamental.

## What problem does it solve?

The exercise tests the DESIGN PROCESS: clarify requirements → estimate scale → choose components → address the hot spots. The steps transfer to any system.

## How it works

Step 1 — clarify before designing: how many new links per day? Reads vastly outnumber writes (one link created, thousands of redirects) — so the system is READ-optimized. Custom aliases? Expiry? Those change the key design.

Step 2 — key generation, know the options:
- Base62-encode an auto-incrementing ID: short, collision-free, but sequential (guessable) and needs a central counter.
- Hash the URL and truncate: no counter, but collisions must be handled.
- A key-generation service handing out pre-made batches: scales cleanly; a bit more machinery.

Step 3 — storage: the access pattern is a pure key → value lookup (shortCode → longUrl). A key–value/NoSQL store fits naturally and scales horizontally; nothing about the problem needs joins.

Step 4 — the read path is the whole game: cache hot codes in Redis (and put a CDN in front) so popular redirects never touch the database. The redirect itself: 301 (permanent — browsers cache it, fewer hits to you, but you lose visibility) vs 302 (temporary — every click reaches you, enabling analytics).

The follow-up everyone gets — click analytics without slowing redirects: never write the click synchronously. Emit an event to a queue and let a consumer aggregate; the redirect stays a cache hit plus a fire-and-forget publish.

## Key terms

- Read/write ratio — the number that shapes the whole design
- Base62 — alphanumeric encoding of an id into a short code
- Hot key caching — Redis + CDN carrying the popular redirects
- 301 vs 302 — cached-permanent vs analytics-friendly-temporary
- Async analytics — count clicks off the critical path

questions.title: CAP theorem
questions.prompt: Explain the CAP theorem. What does it actually force you to choose in a distributed system?
questions.answerStructureHint: Define C/A/P, the partition is a given, so the real choice is consistency vs availability during a partition.

expectedPoints.label: Defines consistency, availability, partition tolerance
expectedPoints.description: C = every read sees the latest write, A = every request gets a response, P = system keeps working despite dropped messages.

expectedPoints.label: Partition is unavoidable, so it's C vs A
expectedPoints.description: Since partitions happen, the real decision under a partition is consistency vs availability.

expectedPoints.label: Grounds it in real systems
expectedPoints.description: Gives a CP vs AP example (e.g. a bank ledger vs a shopping cart / eventually consistent store).

followUps.prompt: Even without a partition there is a trade-off. What is it (the 'else' in PACELC)?

questions.title: Design a URL shortener
questions.prompt: Design a URL shortening service like bit.ly. Walk me through the key decisions.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify scale and read/write ratio, key generation, storage, fast redirects with caching, then analytics/expiry.

expectedPoints.label: Clarifies scale and read/write ratio
expectedPoints.description: Asks about traffic, that reads vastly outnumber writes, custom aliases, expiry.

expectedPoints.label: Key generation strategy
expectedPoints.description: Base62 of an ID / counter, hashing with collision handling, or a key-generation service.

expectedPoints.label: Caching for fast redirects
expectedPoints.description: Cache hot short-codes (and use a CDN) so the redirect lookup is fast under read-heavy load.

expectedPoints.label: Appropriate storage choice
expectedPoints.description: A simple key-value / NoSQL store fits the lookup pattern and scales horizontally.

followUps.prompt: The product wants click analytics per link without slowing down redirects. How would you do it?
